title: Setting Up Django's Password Reset Flow

content.problemStatement:
In this worked out example, using Django's powerful built-in authentication system, we have built a 
complete password reset workflow. 
This means:
1.  Users can request a password reset by entering their email.
2.  They receive an email (which we'll see in our console for now) with a unique reset link.
3.  Clicking the link allows them to set a new password.
4.  They get confirmation that their password has been changed.

### Let's see how this was implemented.
### 1. Email Setup:
Instead of setting up a real email service, we configured Django to send emails to the console during development. So when a user requests a password reset, the email with the reset link appears right in the terminal where the server is running. \
We did this by adding this line to the end of our `settings.py` file:
```python
EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.console.EmailBackend'
```
### 2. URLs Setup:
We included Django’s built-in authentication URLs by adding 
`path('accounts/', include('django.contrib.auth.urls'))` in our main urls.py. \
This gives us several important routes automatically:
- Login
- Logout
- Password reset request
- Password reset done (confirmation)
- Password reset confirm (link in email)
- Password reset complete

Notice in `views.py` that, except for Logout, we have not created any views for the above as they will be provided automatically by Django.
### 3. Templates:
Django’s password reset views look for specific templates inside a registration folder. We created these templates to provide user-friendly pages for:
- Entering the email to reset the password
- Confirming the reset email was sent
- Resetting the password through the unique link
- Showing a success message once the password is changed 

### 4. Login and Logout:
We use Django’s built-in login and logout views. For logout, we made sure to use a POST request via a form, because Django’s logout view disallows GET requests for security reasons.
______________________________________________________________________________________________

Click on **RUN** which will create a user with the credentials: \
Username: `chef` \
Password: `chefpass@123` \
Email: `chef@codechef.com` \
**Open the browser in a new tab**
- As you click on `Forgot Password`, you will be asked to enter the email ID. 
- Provide the above email. 
- Now in the terminal, you will be able to see a password reset email with a **reset link**. 
- Copy that link and paste it on a new browser tab **(Use Incognito mode if you are using Chrome browser as it may not work otherwise due to Chrome's security settings)**
- You will get the option for changing the password. 
- Do it and try logging in with the new password that you created.
